entries.title: Certificate chain built
entries.summary: A certificate chain was constructed for validation.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Cryptographic API (CAPI2) constructs a certificate chain for validation. This event fires for every certificate validation the operating system performs — TLS connections, code signing verification, document signing, etc. At baseline volume it is extremely noisy. A chain build failure indicates a certificate that cannot be verified against a trusted root, which may point to a self-signed cert, a private CA not installed in the trust store, or a revoked/expired certificate. This channel is disabled by default and must be enabled via wevtutil or Event Viewer before events are collected.

prerequisites.description: The CAPI2 operational log channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events are collected.

investigationPivots: Filter for Result=failure — successful chain builds are expected and high volume
SubjectCert issuer from an unknown or private CA may indicate an attacker-controlled certificate authority
Chain build failures for code signing certificates on newly executed binaries are high priority
commonFalsePositives: Any TLS connection or code signing validation generates this event — baseline volume is very high
Self-signed certificates used by internal tools or development environments

keyFields.name: IssuerCert
keyFields.description: The issuer certificate in the chain

keyFields.name: ChainContext
keyFields.description: Context handle for the constructed certificate chain

keyFields.name: Result
keyFields.description: Success or failure with error code for the chain build operation

entries.title: Certificate chain policy verified
entries.summary: A certificate chain was evaluated against a chain policy (e.g., SSL, authenticode).
entries.details: Generated when a constructed certificate chain is evaluated against a specific chain policy — for example, the SSL policy (for TLS), the authenticode policy (for code signing), or the base policy. This event reveals why a certificate was accepted or rejected at the policy level, beyond just chain construction. A chain policy verification failure on an authenticode policy for a recently executed binary indicates unsigned or improperly signed code. In TLS inspection and proxy detection scenarios, a chain policy failure may indicate a root CA mismatch caused by SSL interception. Requires CAPI2 operational log to be enabled.

prerequisites.description: The CAPI2 operational log channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events are collected.

investigationPivots: PolicyOID identifies the context — Authenticode policy failures (1.3.6.1.4.1.311.2.1.29) on recently executed binaries are highest priority
Result error codes (CERT_E_REVOKED, CERT_E_EXPIRED, CERT_E_UNTRUSTEDROOT) indicate specific failure types
CERT_E_UNTRUSTEDROOT on a TLS connection may indicate SSL interception by a proxy not in the machine trust store
commonFalsePositives: Internal TLS services using private CA certificates not in the Windows root store
Expired certificates on older internal systems that have not been renewed

keyFields.name: ChainContext
keyFields.description: Context handle for the certificate chain being evaluated

keyFields.name: PolicyOID
keyFields.description: The chain policy being checked (e.g., SSL, authenticode, base)

keyFields.name: Result
keyFields.description: Success or failure with specific policy error flags

entries.title: CRL or OCSP object retrieved
entries.summary: The crypto subsystem fetched a Certificate Revocation List (CRL) or OCSP response from a URL.
entries.details: Generated when CryptRetrieveObjectByUrl is called to fetch a CRL, OCSP staple, or certificate from a remote URL as part of revocation checking. This event records the URL contacted and the result of the fetch. It fires during any certificate validation that includes revocation checking, including TLS handshakes and code signing verification. Also useful for detecting environments where certificate revocation checking is failing because CRL endpoints are unreachable.

investigationPivots: URL field is the primary pivot — CRL Distribution Points at unknown or attacker-controlled domains indicate a malicious signing certificate
Correlate the URL's domain with threat intel to identify known attacker certificate infrastructure
Result=failure for all CRL fetches indicates the endpoint cannot perform revocation checking — a gap that attackers may exploit
commonFalsePositives: Routine CRL and OCSP fetches for all TLS connections and code signing verifications — very high volume
CRL fetch failures when endpoints are offline or when firewall rules block access to CRL Distribution Points

keyFields.name: URL
keyFields.description: The CRL Distribution Point or OCSP responder URL being contacted

keyFields.name: Result
keyFields.description: Success or failure of the URL fetch operation

entries.title: Certificate revocation check performed
entries.summary: A revocation check was performed against a certificate in a chain.
entries.details: Generated when Windows performs a revocation check (CRL or OCSP) against a specific certificate. A result of CERT_E_REVOKED indicates the certificate is on a revocation list. Adversaries occasionally use certificates that were valid at signing time but have since been revoked, or certificates revoked due to compromise. Requires CAPI2 operational log to be enabled.

prerequisites.description: The CAPI2 operational log channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events are collected.

investigationPivots: CERT_E_REVOKED on a code signing certificate is critical — identify what binary triggered the check and retrieve its hash
CertificateThumbprint links this event to a specific certificate — search for the thumbprint across threat intel databases
Correlate with EID 30 to identify the CRL endpoint that confirmed revocation
commonFalsePositives: Revocation checks against expired or decommissioned internal CA certificates
Applications that embed old signing certificates for legacy compatibility and trigger revocation events on startup

keyFields.name: CertificateThumbprint
keyFields.description: Thumbprint of the certificate being checked for revocation

keyFields.name: CertificateIssuer
keyFields.description: Issuer of the certificate being checked

keyFields.name: RevocationCheckResult
keyFields.description: Result of the revocation check (e.g., CERT_E_REVOKED)

entries.title: X.509 chain policy verified
entries.summary: An X.509 certificate chain was evaluated against a trust or application policy.
entries.details: Generated when an X.509 certificate chain is evaluated against a configured trust or application policy, such as the Basic Constraints policy, Enhanced Key Usage (EKU) policy, or a custom application policy. Failures indicate a certificate is being used outside its intended scope — for example, a certificate with an EKU of 'Client Authentication' being used for 'Code Signing'. In attacks involving stolen or forged certificates, EKU mismatches may be present if the attacker used a certificate not intended for the context in which they deployed it. Requires CAPI2 operational log to be enabled.

prerequisites.description: The CAPI2 operational log channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events are collected.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Subvert Trust Controls: Install Root Certificate

investigationPivots: PolicyOID failures indicating EKU mismatch suggest a certificate being used outside its intended purpose
Combine CertificateThumbprint with EID 11 and EID 13 events for the same certificate to build the full validation picture
A new trusted root CA certificate being installed (visible via EID 70 for basic constraints) warrants immediate investigation
commonFalsePositives: Internal PKI certificates with custom EKUs not matching expected Windows policy checks
Test certificates in development environments with non-standard policy settings

keyFields.name: CertificateThumbprint
keyFields.description: Thumbprint of the certificate being evaluated against the policy

keyFields.name: PolicyOID
keyFields.description: The trust or application policy OID being checked

keyFields.name: Result
keyFields.description: Success or failure of the policy evaluation
